title: Accelerating Quantum Computing Research Using AI and Robotics
description: Chapter 4 of dossier QC-Accelerate-002: 'The loop eats first.' The dossier's thesis is a propose-refute-simulate-score-learn loop; this chapter runs the loop's cheapest case -- a search for quantum error-correcting codes, where 'making' a candidate means simulating it. It is, to the author's knowledge, the first AI-driven code search refereed at circuit level (finite-bias, faulty syndrome extraction) rather than at the flattering code-capacity grade that every published AI code search uses. The referee is a two-mode evaluator anchored to Amazon's published Ocelot cat-qubit chip (three independently measured closures with no fitting) and corridor-validated against an independent published simulation. Four priors were pre-registered and cryptographically timestamped before the search ran; two resolved true, two false. The measured, model-level payload: codes must match their bias regime. A searched [24,4,12] code (three-fold repetition crossed with the [8,4,4] extended Hamming code) beats budget-matched repetition by about four times at the future high-bias operating point, and inverts to 12-63 times worse when the gates are made ten times dirtier -- so the advantage exists only in the regime the search was aimed at. This is a proof of principle: the loop turned once, over a small hand-seeded candidate set, with scoring automated and proposals human/LLM-guided -- not an exhausted search, and explicitly not a hardware demonstration. Ships with the explicit winning code, an eleven-source citation audit, a red-team led by the model-versus-hardware caveat, a ten-entry ledger of the machinery's own caught-and-published errors, and a reproducibility section (noise model, decoder configuration, committed scripts, pinned software stack, and the Ocelot data DOI). An Open Dossier publication: executable claims under CI, published adversarial review, OpenTimestamps-anchored releases.
